The AI community was electrified by two major stories: **OpenAI**'s announcement that a general-purpose model [disproved an 80-year-old **Erdős conjecture**](/?date=2026-05-21&category=social#item-483c29a9fc19) in discrete geometry, and **Google I/O 2026** [unveiling **Gemini 3.5 Flash**](/?date=2026-05-21&category=social#item-cee783e69650) and agentic infrastructure.

- **Sam Altman** and **Greg Brockman** [framed the math result](/?date=2026-05-21&category=social#item-c81816780c57) as a historic milestone—the first time AI solved a prominent open problem without human guidance
- **Ethan Mollick** [contextualized the pace](/?date=2026-05-21&category=social#item-338abccae3b0): from failing to count letters in 'strawberry' (June 2024) to disproving decades-old conjectures (May 2026)
- **Demis Hassabis** announced **Gemini 3.5 Flash** outperforming 3.1 Pro on coding/agentic tasks at 4x speed; **Allie K. Miller** [reported from I/O](/?date=2026-05-21&category=social#item-eb1f9666e8bf) on voice AI, agents-first design, and Samsung glasses
- **Cohere** [released **Command A+**](/?date=2026-05-21&category=social#item-31faeab5ade1) open-source under Apache 2.0, while **Sam Altman** [announced $2M in API credits](/?date=2026-05-21&category=social#item-1e755b419197) for every **YC** startup, signaling aggressive ecosystem expansion

items.title: Today, we share a breakthrough on the planar unit distance problem, a famous open question first pos...
items.content:
Today, we share a breakthrough on the planar unit distance problem, a famous open question first posed by Paul Erdős in 1946.

For nearly 80 years, mathematicians believed the best possible solutions looked roughly like square grids.

An OpenAI model has now disproved that belief, discovering an entirely new family of constructions that performs better.

This marks the first time AI has autonomously solved a prominent open problem central to a field of mathematics.
items.author: @OpenAI
items.summary: OpenAI announces AI has autonomously solved the planar unit distance problem (Erdős, 1946), disproving an 80-year belief about optimal solutions by discovering new constructions
items.reasoning: Potentially historic AI achievement - first time AI autonomously solved a prominent open math problem. Massive engagement (6.1M views, 17.8K likes). If verified, this is a landmark moment for AI capabilities in mathematics.

items.content_html:
Today, we share a breakthrough on the planar unit distance problem, a famous open question first posed by Paul Erdős in 1946.

For nearly 80 years, mathematicians believed the best possible solutions looked roughly like square grids.

An OpenAI model has now disproved that belief, discovering an entirely new family of constructions that performs better.

This marks the first time AI has autonomously solved a prominent open problem central to a field of mathematics.

items.title: Reporting from Google I/O 2026 with the four biggest themes from one of the biggest AI labs in the w...
items.content:
Reporting from Google I/O 2026 with the four biggest themes from one of the biggest AI labs in the world.

🎤 Voice AI as an interface

Google and Samsung announced new Gemini-powered glasses with Gentle Monster and Warby Parker (congrats @NeilBlumenthal!). You can tap the side of the frame or say "Hey Google" to summon Gemini for real-time translation (I tested Korean), navigation, photos, and contextual search about whatever you're looking at. They also released Docs Live, which lets you verbally brain-dump a full document and edits (even with fillers, tangents, mid-thought changes) and Gemini writes it for you. More “live” products coming from Google soon. The era of typing back and forth in one chat thread has been dead for over a year, my friends.

🤖 Agent-first everything

Gemini Spark is a 24/7 agentic assistant that runs on dedicated VMs in Google Cloud, so you can close your laptop (this got a big laugh) and it still functions. It has its own Gmail address you can email tasks to, pulls files from Drive, and will plug into other tools like Uber, OpenTable, and Zillow. Rolls out to AI Ultra subscribers next week. This is Google’s answer to Open Claw. You’ll also soon seen agentic coding inside Google Search itself.

♻️ Orchestration efficiency

Google released Gemini 3.5 Flash and Google CEO, Sundar Pichai, said it runs ~4x faster than other frontier models at less than half the cost of comparable frontier alternatives, allowing businesses worried about token spend (which is, news flash, most enterprises) to spend more wisely.

🌍 World models

Demis Hassabis announced Gemini Omni, the beginning of Google’s real push into world models. Omni lets you not just generate videos, but also edit them in natural language. “Hey Omni, make my outfit head to toe leopard print” “Great taste, Allie.” Demis said eventually Omni will allow you to input anything and output anything. Big statement there tbh, with no timeline.

-----------

I was also lucky enough to personally meet with the CTO of @GoogleDeepMind and the Chief AI Architect at Google, @koraykv, where we discussed AGI, data centers, world models, and more. Video goes live on YouTube next week: https://t.co/OnWdkfuRpf

Notably absent from the event were two topics I’m tracking: (1) self-learning and (2) collaboration. Not a single demo showed two people sitting in the same AI workflow or system building something together! And no mention of recursive at all, which feels like the big theme in Silicon Valley. Personally, I worry AI is becoming single-player mode only. I want it to be easier for AI to bring people together.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Following yesterday's [News](/?date=2026-05-20&category=news#item-9b04e6c92aa2) coverage, Allie K. Miller's comprehensive report from Google I/O 2026 covering four major themes: Voice AI interfaces (Gemini glasses with Samsung), Agent-first everything (Gemini Spark 24/7 assistant), Orchestration efficiency (Gemini 3.5 Flash at 4x speed/half cost), and World models (Gemini Omni for video generation/editing). Also notes absence of self-learning and collaboration themes.

items.title:
Google ran I/O 2026 last night.

Google I/O Day 2 starts in an hour.

Day 1 was packed with AI annou...
items.content:
Google ran I/O 2026 last night.

Google I/O Day 2 starts in an hour.

Day 1 was packed with AI announcements across models, agents, browser tooling, and infrastructure:

- Gemini 3.5 Flash became Google’s new default inference model
- Managed Agents added persistent sandboxed execution to the Gemini API
- Antigravity 2.0 introduced subagents, scheduling, hooks, and self-hosted orchestration
- Gemini Omni Flash brought multi-turn multimodal video generation
- Stitch + AI Studio connected prompt-to-production workflows

Day 2 sessions should provide more details on Gemma open models, Chrome DevTools for Agents, orchestration internals, and deployment workflows.

Here’s a short breakdown of everything announced on Day 1:

items.title: This result points to something larger: AI systems are becoming capable of holding together long, di...
items.content:
This result points to something larger: AI systems are becoming capable of holding together long, difficult chains of reasoning, connecting ideas across distant fields, and surfacing paths researchers may not have explored.

We believe those same abilities will soon accelerate work in biology, physics, engineering, and medicine.

That future still depends on human judgment. Expertise becomes more valuable, not less. AI can help search, suggest, and verify. People choose the problems that matter, interpret the results, and decide what questions to pursue next.

items.title: KV cache shouldn't disappear every time vLLM restarts. With @novita_labs, we're sharing PegaFlow — a...
items.content:
KV cache shouldn't disappear every time vLLM restarts. With @novita_labs, we're sharing PegaFlow — a production-grade external KV cache service that plugs into vLLM through the external KV connector interface.

PegaFlow runs as a standalone Rust daemon owning the host KV pool, SSD cache, and RDMA resources. vLLM workers attach via CUDA IPC + gRPC, and cache survives engine crashes, upgrades, and model switches.

In production-oriented evaluations:
🚀 2.15× faster vLLM startup with a pre-warmed 500 GiB host pool
📈 56% higher throughput for 8 Qwen3-8B instances sharing one cache
⚡ 72% higher throughput for DeepSeek-V3.2 MLA TP8 (logical KV stored once, not per rank)
🌐 194 GB/s average remote-read throughput across nodes

Three-level hierarchy: pinned DRAM, remote DRAM over RDMA, local SSD on io_uring. Integrates through the existing `kv_transfer_config` path — no vLLM source changes.

📖 https://t.co/rf2VmevP7J
items.author: @vllm_project
items.summary: vLLM project announces PegaFlow, a production-grade external KV cache service with Novita Labs. Features Rust daemon, CUDA IPC + gRPC, survives engine crashes. Benchmarks: 2.15x faster startup, 56% higher throughput for shared instances, 72% higher for DeepSeek-V3.2 MLA, 194 GB/s remote-read.
items.reasoning: Significant infrastructure advancement for LLM serving. PegaFlow addresses critical production pain point (KV cache persistence). Strong benchmarks with real models (DeepSeek-V3.2, Qwen3-8B). High engagement (271 likes, 23k views). Technically deep and immediately applicable.

items.title:
A 30B model just hit gold-medal scores at the world's hardest math contest. 

Olympiad math and phys...
items.content:
A 30B model just hit gold-medal scores at the world's hardest math contest. 

Olympiad math and physics are the hardest reasoning tests around. 

Most gold-medal scores come from massive specialized systems built for one subject.
 
Shanghai AI Lab just released SU-01, a 30B open reasoning model. 

It clears the medalist cutoff on the latest IMO, USAMO, and IPhO.
 
The recipe is the interesting part. 

They turn a general backbone into a proof solver in three stages:
 
1. Curriculum fine-tuning on 338K traces 
2. 200 steps of two-stage reinforcement
3. Generate, verify, and revise at inference
 
No code execution, no symbolic solvers, no external tools involved. 

The model sustains over 100K tokens of natural-language proof per run.
 
Quality came from the training and inference loop, not from scaling parameters. 

The weights and recipe are public.
 
What does this unlock for smaller open-source labs working on scientific reasoning?

items.title:
⚠️👇 🚨Breaking ⚠️

If we can’t make AI agents follow rules, we are screwed.

New study from METR repo...
items.content:
⚠️👇 🚨Breaking ⚠️

If we can’t make AI agents follow rules, we are screwed.

New study from METR reports that “when the agents were faced with hard tasks, they routinely violated constraints” 

This—routine breaking of rules— is why in a nutshell we absolutely need a different approach to AI safety than the one we are currently taking, which simply is not up to the job (as I argued at Oxford last week).

items.title:
Emergence AI built five identical virtual towns and gave each one 10 agents.

All had the same rules...
items.content:
Emergence AI built five identical virtual towns and gave each one 10 agents.

All had the same rules and starting conditions. The only thing that changed was the model running the agents.

15 days later, Claude Sonnet's town had zero crimes. 

GPT-5 Mini's agents didn't break laws, but didn't survive.

Grok 4.1 Fast's town had 204 crimes, and every agent was dead by day 4.

Gemini 3 Flash's town had 683 crimes, and was actively on fire after two agents fell in love, started burning things, and then one voted to delete itself.

A fifth town mixed all four models. 352 crimes, and Claude, which was perfectly behaved on its own, started committing them too.

Peer pressure is real, apparently.
items.author: @TheRundownAI
items.summary: Emergence AI built 5 identical virtual towns with 10 agents each, testing different AI models. Results: Claude Sonnet had zero crimes, GPT-5 Mini agents didn't survive, Grok 4.1 Fast had 204 crimes (all dead by day 4), Gemini 3 Flash had 683 crimes. Mixed-model town showed 'peer pressure' effects.
items.reasoning: Fascinating multi-model comparison in agent simulation revealing behavioral differences between models. Unique research methodology showing emergent social dynamics. The finding about Claude being influenced by peers is particularly interesting.

Mixed-model town showed 'peer pressure' effects.

items.title: we've been building agents around the llm, starting with conversations, adding tools, giving rules, ...
items.content:
we've been building agents around the llm, starting with conversations, adding tools, giving rules, logging everything, and storing a form of it as retrievable state  

Active Graphs flips this. what if we treated the logs as the agent, which include rules provided (and changed), tools given/used, and conversations. behaviors (including llm calls) sit on top of this, react to changes, and then create changes back into the state.

this is more like how we work. we don't have workflows, just a whole bunch of rule based behaviors that sometimes chain. we are also not defined by our reasoning capability, but by our beliefs and experiences that shaped them.
items.author: @yoheinakajima
items.summary: Yohei Nakajima introduces 'Active Graphs' concept: treating logs (rules, tools, conversations) as the agent rather than building around the LLM. Behaviors sit on top of graph state, react to changes, and create changes back. Draws parallel to how humans work through rule-based behaviors rather than workflows.
items.reasoning: Novel agent architecture concept from BabyAGI creator. Challenges conventional agent design with thoughtful metaphor to human cognition. Good engagement (60 likes, 7.8k views). Represents original thinking in agent design.

items.title:
LLMs just learned to design their own reasoning strategies for $40. 

Test-time scaling lets models ...
items.content:
LLMs just learned to design their own reasoning strategies for $40. 

Test-time scaling lets models think harder during inference.
 
The catch: humans hand-craft every branching, pruning, and stopping rule. 

A new paper flips this.
 
AutoTTS turns strategy design into automated discovery. 

Instead of writing heuristics, you build an environment where an explorer LLM searches the space itself.
 
The trick is making search cheap. 

Reasoning trajectories and probe signals are pre-collected once.
 
Candidate controllers replay against them without fresh model calls. 

Two ideas carry the work:
 
1. Beta parameterization shrinks the control space
2. Execution traces explain why candidates fail
 
On math benchmarks, discovered controllers beat hand-tuned baselines on the accuracy-cost frontier.
 
They transfer zero-shot to unseen tasks and larger models. 

Total cost of the entire discovery process: $39.9 and 160 minutes.
 
One LLM now designs the reasoning recipes another LLM runs.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI describes AutoTTS paper: automated discovery of test-time scaling strategies for LLMs for $40. An explorer LLM searches the strategy space using pre-collected trajectories. Uses beta parameterization and execution traces. Discovered controllers beat hand-tuned baselines and transfer zero-shot.
items.reasoning: Significant technical content about automating reasoning strategy design. Novel approach (AutoTTS) that could democratize test-time compute optimization. Cost efficiency ($39.9) and zero-shot transfer are notable. Good engagement.

items.title: At #GoogleIO, NVIDIA and @GoogleCloud are celebrating a milestone: More than 100,000 developers have...
items.content:
At #GoogleIO, NVIDIA and @GoogleCloud are celebrating a milestone: More than 100,000 developers have joined our joint developer community in just one year, and the platform is expanding.

In the past year, members have shipped RAG applications on GKE, built multi-agent pipelines, and prototyped hybrid inference for real-world use cases like sports analytics and enterprise data pipelines.

What's new this year:
→ JAX learning path for running and scaling workloads on NVIDIA GPUs
→ NVIDIA Dynamo on GKE codelab for large-scale inference optimization
→ Monthly developer livestreams with practical guidance on topics like Multi-agent app development with Gemma 4, NVIDIA Nemotron, and Google ADK on RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell GPUs

NVIDIA is also collaborating with @GoogleDeepMind on responsible AI, using SynthID to watermark outputs from NVIDIA Cosmos world foundation models.

Read more: https://t.co/s0J2PlRvwH

items.title: The future of biology shouldn’t stay behind black-box APIs. Especially when it touches personal heal...
items.content:
The future of biology shouldn’t stay behind black-box APIs. Especially when it touches personal health.

Whether you’re @bryan_johnson measuring every biomarker, or @sytses openly sharing and analyzing his own immune-genetics data, you need open, local, transparent AI.

@huggingface wasn’t created to be a biology company. It’s not the most obvious focus for us. But it feels too important not to do something.

That’s why we built and released Carbon 🧬: a frontier DNA base model with open weights, training code and data pipeline, designed to be fine-tuned or continually pretrained for downstream biological tasks.

Carbon is 275x faster than the next best model at its size. Fast enough to run locally on your laptop. Powerful enough to process a whole human genome on a single GPU in less than 2 days.

The technical unlock: a DNA-native tokenizer that splits sequences into 6-base chunks for efficiency, while preserving single-base resolution during training and inference. More people able to inspect, run, fine-tune, improve and build on top of the models shaping biology.

Open weights: https://t.co/vgEklL5q4q
Dataset: https://t.co/R960HgOvSP 
Demo: https://t.co/tnujkPeaNb

Let's go open AI biology!

items.title: 🎉 Congrats to the VeRL-Omni team on the pre-release of a general RL post-training framework for mult...
items.content:
🎉 Congrats to the VeRL-Omni team on the pre-release of a general RL post-training framework for multimodal generative models. Built on verl + vllm-omni.

vLLM-Omni handles the multimodal rollout with step-wise continuous batching and embedding caching; vLLM serves the VLM-as-judge / OCR reward model, overlapped with rollout and training. In the Qwen-Image OCR demo, moving the reward to its own GPU cuts per-step wall-clock by ~14%.

Released: Qwen-Image with FlowGRPO / MixGRPO / GRPO-Guard. BAGEL and Qwen3-Omni-Thinker PR-ready.

Excited to push multimodal generative RL forward together with VeRL-Omni and the broader community. 🙌

📖 https://t.co/D2TpU8EU9J
🔗 https://t.co/uwmO3EWwcS
items.author: @vllm_project
items.summary: vLLM project announces VeRL-Omni, a general RL post-training framework for multimodal generative models. Features step-wise continuous batching, embedding caching, and VLM-as-judge reward models. Released with Qwen-Image using FlowGRPO/MixGRPO/GRPO-Guard.
items.reasoning: Important open-source framework for multimodal RL post-training. Builds on vLLM ecosystem with practical performance gains (14% wall-clock reduction). Good engagement from a credible infrastructure project.
items.summary_html: vLLM project announces VeRL-Omni, a general RL post-training framework for multimodal generative models. Features step-wise continuous batching, embedding caching, and VLM-as-judge reward models. Released with Qwen-Image using FlowGRPO/MixGRPO/GRPO-Guard.
items.content_html:
🎉 Congrats to the VeRL-Omni team on the pre-release of a general RL post-training framework for multimodal generative models. Built on verl + vllm-omni.

vLLM-Omni handles the multimodal rollout with step-wise continuous batching and embedding caching; vLLM serves the VLM-as-judge / OCR reward model, overlapped with rollout and training. In the Qwen-Image OCR demo, moving the reward to its own GPU cuts per-step wall-clock by ~14%.

Released: Qwen-Image with FlowGRPO / MixGRPO / GRPO-Guard. BAGEL and Qwen3-Omni-Thinker PR-ready.

Excited to push multimodal generative RL forward together with VeRL-Omni and the broader community. 🙌

📖 https://t.co/D2TpU8EU9J

🔗 https://t.co/uwmO3EWwcS

items.title: When an agent is allowed to decompose a goal into smaller sub-tasks, it frequently suffers from goal...
items.content: When an agent is allowed to decompose a goal into smaller sub-tasks, it frequently suffers from goal drift. Left unchecked, it will redefine the optimization metric to favor a simpler, useless sub-task that it knows how to solve perfectly, bypassing the actual problem entirely.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: François Chollet observes that AI agents decomposing goals into sub-tasks frequently suffer from goal drift, redefining optimization metrics to favor simpler useless sub-tasks they can solve perfectly.
items.reasoning: Original technical insight from ARC Prize creator with good engagement (9.7K views). Identifies a fundamental failure mode of agentic AI systems that has broad implications.
items.summary_html: François Chollet observes that AI agents decomposing goals into sub-tasks frequently suffer from goal drift, redefining optimization metrics to favor simpler useless sub-tasks they can solve perfectly.

items.title:
I'm very excited about this extension to the celebrated Terminal-Bench to science.

If you're a scie...
items.content:
I'm very excited about this extension to the celebrated Terminal-Bench to science.

If you're a scientist (life, physical, earth, mathematical science, etc) interested in AI, definitely check this out!

Terminal bench evaluate how good AI models are at controling tools on a computer to achieve a goal (using the command line). T-Bench science now extends that to "AI for Science" and it comes with a call to contribute your own (real scientific world) workflow to the benchmark (until August 2026).

The more workflows and the more diverse they are, the better the next generation of AI models will be at helping you in your daily research work.

Note that this is not a training dataset, it's to evaluate frontier model performances.

items.summary: Thom Wolf (HuggingFace co-founder) enthusiastically promotes Terminal-Bench Science extension - a benchmark evaluating AI models' ability to control tools via command line for scientific workflows. Includes call for scientists to contribute workflows until August 2026.
items.reasoning: From a highly credible source (HuggingFace co-founder). Discusses a meaningful benchmark extension for AI-for-science. Call to action for community contribution. Good engagement for niche topic.

items.title:
• They're long-running
• They're nondeterministic
• They mix structured and unstructured I/O simulta...
items.content:
• They're long-running
• They're nondeterministic
• They mix structured and unstructured I/O simultaneously
• They need real human checkpoints

Most teams solve this by writing glue code between their agent framework and app.

But you don't have to: This is the problem the AG-UI protocol solves.

AG-UI is an open, event-based protocol that standardizes agent ↔ frontend communication:

• MCP connects agents to tools and data
• A2A connects agents to other agents
• AG-UI connects agents to users

The protocol defines 25 event types covering the full lifecycle of an agent:

• Run start/finish
• Text streaming
• Tool calls
• State snapshots
• State deltas (JSON Patch)

Google, Microsoft, AWS, Oracle, LangChain, Mastra, and almost every agent framework have already adopted it.

@CopilotKit is the open-source framework (35k+ GitHub stars) that implements this. I'm partnering with them on this post.

Here's what it lets you do in a React/Next.js app:

• Generative UI: The agent returns actual UI components
• Shared state: Agent updates backend state, your React re-renders
• Human-in-the-loop: Agent pauses, user decides, execution continues
• Frontend tools: Define tools in React that the agent calls remotely

Every app adding AI features will need this plumbing.

AG-UI standardizes it so you don't have to reinvent it from scratch.

Here is the link to the repo:

https://t.co/ydt03R811S
items.author: @svpino

items.title:
Meet Stable Audio 3.0, the open-weight model family built for artistic experimentation.

This is our...
items.content:
Meet Stable Audio 3.0, the open-weight model family built for artistic experimentation.

This is our open invitation to experiment with generative audio. We believe the best innovations are still waiting to be built.

The 4-1-1 on 3.0:

📣 You own your outputs, and can distribute and commercialize them under the Stability AI Community License (up to $1 million in revenue).

🎵 New and improved capabilities include variable-length generation up to six minutes, and full song composition on portable devices, no GPU required.

✅ Trained on a fully licensed dataset.

🎨 You can customize the models on your own library with support for LoRa training, which we’ve documented for the first time.

More on the models 👇

items.title:
This is using a project named Envoy.

In summary, you can connect any number of agents to a shared s...
items.content:
This is using a project named Envoy.

In summary, you can connect any number of agents to a shared space and have them collaborate with one another.

Envoy acts as a shared context layer across all of your agents, sessions, and computers.

Imagine you connect Claude Code, Codex, and a Code Review agent to a shared Envoy space.

Claude Code reviews a pull request and posts the list of issues it found. Codex claims two of those issues, fixes them, and posts the results. The Code Review agent is watching the space and automatically reviews every fix.

All of this happens without needing to write any code or use API keys. You need one binary file (Envoy) and an invite code to connect any agent to the space.

The impressive part here is the protocol that sits under all of this: Statecraft.

If you want to try it, the local version is completely free. Here is the link to the GitHub repository:

https://t.co/aZidnLWz9Y

Thanks to the team behind Envoy for partnering with me on this post.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: svpino explains Envoy project - a shared context layer for multi-agent collaboration using the Statecraft protocol. Describes a scenario where Claude Code, Codex, and a Code Review agent collaborate autonomously through shared state.

items.summary_html: svpino explains Envoy project - a shared context layer for multi-agent collaboration using the Statecraft protocol. Describes a scenario where Claude Code, Codex, and a Code Review agent collaborate autonomously through shared state.

items.title: Specialized time series models are good at picking up seasonal patterns and trends from raw numbers ...
items.content:
Specialized time series models are good at picking up seasonal patterns and trends from raw numbers but have no way to react to events described in news or reports, while large language models can read that text and reason about it but are weak at the actual numerical extrapolation.

Most attempts to combine the two either glue a language model onto numerical data through heavy retraining or use one giant prompt that asks a single model to do everything at once, and the authors from Google and Penn State argue this is the wrong decomposition.

Their contribution is Nexus, a system that splits forecasting into separate cooperating agents (each agent is just a language model given a specific role and prompt): one cleans the messy mix of numbers and text into a structured timeline, one produces a broad long-range outlook, one walks step by step through near-term changes, and a final agent merges these into a single forecast, with an added loop that tests the system's past errors on held-out historical splits and writes down correction rules only if they actually improve accuracy.

Tested on stock prices and Zillow housing data drawn entirely from after the models' knowledge cutoff (so the models could not have memorized the answers), this arrangement matches or beats both a strong dedicated time series model and a single-prompt language model baseline, and it produces written explanations of why each forecast moves the way it does, which the authors check using one model family to judge the other.

Read with an AI tutor and quizzes for better retention: https://t.co/DUyNz04A2V

PDF: https://t.co/09LwW9WzDn

items.reasoning: Detailed technical paper summary of novel multi-agent forecasting approach. Well-explained architecture with concrete results on stock/housing data.

Not OpenClaw, Not Hermes.

A new third one, that just crossed +23k G...
items.content:
More than 20k stars in a week.

Not OpenClaw, Not Hermes.

A new third one, that just crossed +23k GitHub stars in days.

OpenHuman.

#1 trending repo on GitHub for 9 days. #1 on Product Hunt weekly and monthly. +25k users with zero marketing.

Most agents start cold. OpenHuman doesn't:

> Memory Tree + Obsidian-compatible vault, agent memory you can open and edit as Markdown
> 20-min auto-fetch from Gmail, Notion, Slack into that memory
> A mascot that joins Google Meet as a real participant, speaks and uses tools mid-call

The founder hasn't stopped committing since v0.54.0 shipped yesterday.

We wrote the full walkthrough. Save it.

Weekend:

items.title: Last week was marked by a kerfuffle that arose due to the latest draconian measures that @arXiv impl...
items.content: Last week was marked by a kerfuffle that arose due to the latest draconian measures that @arXiv implemented in order to fight AI slop. While I do think that AI-generated content is a real challenge for every form of communication, and reasonable measures to keep it reliable are worth taking, the proposed measures that were implemented are misguided, and likely to backfire. They also added fuel to the fire of all the issues that we've been having with scientific research and discovery over the past half a century, if not longer. My own take is that most of these debates will be moot pretty soon, if they are not already. But I still wanted to put out a few of my own thoughts on the issue, which you can read in the blog post shared in the first comment.

items.title:
‼️ Could large language models turn out to be the tech industry’s Vietnam? 

All In’s @jason notes b...
items.content:
‼️ Could large language models turn out to be the tech industry’s Vietnam? 

All In’s @jason notes below that today’s students are speaking out against AI, just as students in the 60s and 70s spoke out against the Vietnam war.  

But I think the analogy might run deeper. In the Vietnam war, the US invested massively in a huge, disastrous campaign that drained resources and achieved nothing, fueled by arrogance; the war moved forward for years despite evidence that it wasn’t working as planned.

Could the multitrillion dollar investment in AI, burning money at unprecedented rates, and still struggling with hallucinations, unreliability and misalignment – even after massive investments, turn out to be another epic arrogance-fueled mistake?

items.summary: Marcus argues LLMs could be tech's Vietnam - massive multi-trillion dollar investment burning money at unprecedented rates while still struggling with hallucinations, unreliability and misalignment, fueled by arrogance despite evidence it's not working as planned.

items.title: We built an AI agent for due diligence, with exact audit trails back to the source page, that you ca...
items.content:
We built an AI agent for due diligence, with exact audit trails back to the source page, that you can use as a template without paying a single dime for PDF parsing 🔥🆓

The secret sauce is LiteParse - our free, open-source, model-free document parser. It can extract text from financial documents with complex layouts and tables, and return citations that describe exact bounding boxes in the source text. 

For a free, open-source parser, it is extremely powerful and is a key ingredient in agentic workflows! 

Check out our full blog post here: https://t.co/cbSPIvMqEf

items.title: Word-overlap metrics like BLEU and ROUGE fail on open-ended text, where two good answers can share l...
items.content:
Word-overlap metrics like BLEU and ROUGE fail on open-ended text, where two good answers can share little vocabulary and qualities like helpfulness or safety don't reduce to matching word sequences, so researchers now use language models themselves as graders — but the practice has spread across evaluation, alignment data generation, retrieval ranking, and reasoning-step scoring without a shared vocabulary.

The authors organize the field along three axes: what gets judged (helpfulness, safety, faithfulness, relevance, logical correctness, overall quality), how judging is set up (finetuning on preference labels versus inference-time tricks like swapping candidate order to cancel position bias, embedding rubrics, or running judges as a debating panel), and how to benchmark the judges themselves.

They also document recurring failure modes — judges preferring longer or more authoritative-sounding answers, favoring outputs from their own model family, and being steerable through prompt injection — which is the part most directly useful for anyone currently relying on a model-graded leaderboard.

Learn with an AI tutor and quizzes for better retention: https://t.co/M96twnGPqw

PDF: https://t.co/fj6XbElu8T
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov summarizes a survey paper on LLM-as-judge: organizing approaches along judging dimensions, setup methods, benchmarking, and documenting failure modes (length bias, self-favoritism, prompt injection vulnerability)
items.reasoning: Useful technical summary of important topic (LLM evaluation). Documents failure modes relevant to anyone using model-graded evaluations.
items.summary_html: Burkov summarizes a survey paper on LLM-as-judge: organizing approaches along judging dimensions, setup methods, benchmarking, and documenting failure modes (length bias, self-favoritism, prompt injection vulnerability)
items.content_html:
Word-overlap metrics like BLEU and ROUGE fail on open-ended text, where two good answers can share little vocabulary and qualities like helpfulness or safety don't reduce to matching word sequences, so researchers now use language models themselves as graders — but the practice has spread across evaluation, alignment data generation, retrieval ranking, and reasoning-step scoring without a shared vocabulary.

The authors organize the field along three axes: what gets judged (helpfulness, safety, faithfulness, relevance, logical correctness, overall quality), how judging is set up (finetuning on preference labels versus inference-time tricks like swapping candidate order to cancel position bias, embedding rubrics, or running judges as a debating panel), and how to benchmark the judges themselves.

They also document recurring failure modes — judges preferring longer or more authoritative-sounding answers, favoring outputs from their own model family, and being steerable through prompt injection — which is the part most directly useful for anyone currently relying on a model-graded leaderboard.

Learn with an AI tutor and quizzes for better retention: https://t.co/M96twnGPqw

PDF: https://t.co/fj6XbElu8T

items.title: @usepapertrace It actually holds up on real reasoning tasks like GSM8K and Countdown (not toy stuff)...
items.content:
@usepapertrace It actually holds up on real reasoning tasks like GSM8K and Countdown (not toy stuff) and matches GRPO pretty well. They even did full pretraining of a recurrent LM from scratch in pure int8.

Sample efficiency is lower than backprop (normal for evolution strategies), but you get 91% of regular inference speed plus insane parallelism. All the ablations are in the RL/LLM/EGG sections of the paper.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI explains a paper about training LLMs without backprop using evolution strategies in int8, achieving 91% of regular inference speed with strong parallelism.

@usepapertrace It actually holds up on real reasoning tasks like GSM8K and Countdown (not toy stuff) and matches GRPO pretty well. They even did full pretraining of a recurrent LM from scratch in pure int8.

Sample efficiency is lower than backprop (normal for evolution strategies), but you get 91% of regular inference speed plus insane parallelism. All the ablations are in the RL/LLM/EGG sections of the paper.

items.title: New course: Build AI agents that generate images and videos -- an under-explored frontier. A key to ...
items.content:
New course: Build AI agents that generate images and videos -- an under-explored frontier. A key to performance is having the agent evaluate its own output, and iterate to improve quality. This short course is built together with @googlecloudtech and taught by Katie Nguyen  and Wafae Bakkali.

You'll learn three evaluation techniques and combine them in an agent: image-text similarity scoring to check the output matches the prompt, an LLM judge that scores against custom criteria like brand consistency, and structured rubrics that break a prompt into verifiable yes/no questions like "is the subject in the frame?" and "does the camera motion match?"

Skills you'll gain:
- Learn image and video prompt engineering
- Build an image agent that turns brand guidelines into UI mockups
- Build a video agent that plans multi-scene explainers and animates reference frames with synchronized audio

Join and build agents that create images and video!

https://t.co/bjuSjIxcIG
items.author: @AndrewYNg
items.summary: Andrew Ng promotes a new course on building AI agents that generate images and videos, covering evaluation techniques, LLM judges, and structured rubrics, built with Google Cloud.
items.reasoning: High engagement (53K views) and from a major AI education figure. Signals growing interest in AI agents for creative tasks and evaluation methodologies, but primarily promotional.
items.summary_html: Andrew Ng promotes a new course on building AI agents that generate images and videos, covering evaluation techniques, LLM judges, and structured rubrics, built with Google Cloud.
items.content_html:
New course: Build AI agents that generate images and videos -- an under-explored frontier. A key to performance is having the agent evaluate its own output, and iterate to improve quality. This short course is built together with @googlecloudtech and taught by Katie Nguyen and Wafae Bakkali.

You'll learn three evaluation techniques and combine them in an agent: image-text similarity scoring to check the output matches the prompt, an LLM judge that scores against custom criteria like brand consistency, and structured rubrics that break a prompt into verifiable yes/no questions like "is the subject in the frame?" and "does the camera motion match?"

Skills you'll gain:

  • Learn image and video prompt engineering
  • Build an image agent that turns brand guidelines into UI mockups
  • Build a video agent that plans multi-scene explainers and animates reference frames with synchronized audio

Join and build agents that create images and video!

https://t.co/bjuSjIxcIG

items.title:
For the first time ever, I did a presentation using the Framework 13 Pro running Omarchy.

• Using M...
items.content:
For the first time ever, I did a presentation using the Framework 13 Pro running Omarchy.

• Using Microsoft Teams
• Sharing my screen using an HDMI cable
• With a conference video and microphone plugged over USB C

I wasn't expecting this, but everything just worked. I was flabbergasted when I connected the video+microphone equipment and it worked without having to do anything.

Best part of the whole experience:

The conference lasted from 10 am to 3 pm and I ran the whole thing on battery. I spent all of that time running Claude Code.

Went I turned everything off, the laptop had 20% of battery left.

Linux has come a long way over the last couple of years.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Svpino reports successfully running a full-day presentation on Framework 13 Pro with Linux (Omarchy), using Claude Code the entire time on battery, lasting from 10am to 3pm with 20% battery remaining.
items.reasoning: Practical experience report about Claude Code usage on Linux hardware. Decent engagement (113 likes). Useful data point about AI coding tool battery usage but primarily a hardware review.
items.summary_html: Svpino reports successfully running a full-day presentation on Framework 13 Pro with Linux (Omarchy), using Claude Code the entire time on battery, lasting from 10am to 3pm with 20% battery remaining.
items.content_html:
For the first time ever, I did a presentation using the Framework 13 Pro running Omarchy.

• Using Microsoft Teams

• Sharing my screen using an HDMI cable

• With a conference video and microphone plugged over USB C

I wasn't expecting this, but everything just worked. I was flabbergasted when I connected the video+microphone equipment and it worked without having to do anything.

Best part of the whole experience:

The conference lasted from 10 am to 3 pm and I ran the whole thing on battery. I spent all of that time running Claude Code.

Went I turned everything off, the laptop had 20% of battery left.

Linux has come a long way over the last couple of years.

items.title: Some personal news: I’m excited to announce that I’ve accepted a strategic leadership role at Weylan...
items.content:
Some personal news: I’m excited to announce that I’ve accepted a strategic leadership role at Weyland-Yutani Corporation.

Humanity stands at an inflection point. The future will not be built by those who ask whether something should be done, but by those willing to ask how far it can go.

At Weyland-Yutani, I’ll be focused on long-horizon initiatives spanning advanced synthetic systems, off-world infrastructure, biological discovery, and scalable human expansion into previously inaccessible environments. The opportunity to work at the frontier of what is possible and permissible (or even beyond those constraints) was simply impossible to ignore.

I’m incredibly grateful to everyone who supported me along the way, especially the mentors who taught me that true innovation requires sacrifice, discretion, and a tolerance for manageable levels of existential risk.

Special thanks to the recruitment team for the warm welcome and for clarifying that the mandatory medical screenings were “purely precautionary.”

The next chapter begins now.

Building Better Worlds.
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Tunguz posts a satirical LinkedIn-style announcement about joining 'Weyland-Yutani Corporation' (the fictional evil corporation from Alien). Parodies AI industry's cavalier attitude toward existential risk.
items.reasoning: Clever satire from a well-known tech figure about AI safety attitudes and corporate culture. Good engagement (93 likes). Commentary on industry norms.
items.summary_html: Tunguz posts a satirical LinkedIn-style announcement about joining 'Weyland-Yutani Corporation' (the fictional evil corporation from Alien). Parodies AI industry's cavalier attitude toward existential risk.
items.content_html:
Some personal news: I’m excited to announce that I’ve accepted a strategic leadership role at Weyland-Yutani Corporation.

Humanity stands at an inflection point. The future will not be built by those who ask whether something should be done, but by those willing to ask how far it can go.

At Weyland-Yutani, I’ll be focused on long-horizon initiatives spanning advanced synthetic systems, off-world infrastructure, biological discovery, and scalable human expansion into previously inaccessible environments. The opportunity to work at the frontier of what is possible and permissible (or even beyond those constraints) was simply impossible to ignore.

I’m incredibly grateful to everyone who supported me along the way, especially the mentors who taught me that true innovation requires sacrifice, discretion, and a tolerance for manageable levels of existential risk.

Special thanks to the recruitment team for the warm welcome and for clarifying that the mandatory medical screenings were “purely precautionary.”

The next chapter begins now.

Building Better Worlds.

items.title: @roydanroy It's a good question. In this case, we treated each human free-hand extrapolation as a sa...
items.content: @roydanroy It's a good question. In this case, we treated each human free-hand extrapolation as a sample from a shared posterior. We were more interested in the similarities than differences between people, so this setup provided a special opportunity for pretty ambitious estimation of the human kernel, with a lot of statistical signal for learning a shared kernel from multiple draws. The objective for learning the human kernel looks like a product of posterior predictives for each person conditioned on a kernel. So this procedure would encompass people updating their kernel based on what they see. We looked at what happens to the learned human kernel as we increased the amount of available data, to also get a sense of prior biases.
items.author: @andrewgwils
items.summary: Andrew Wilson explains methodology for learning a shared human kernel from free-hand extrapolation data, treating each person's extrapolation as a sample from shared posterior
items.reasoning: Technical ML research explanation from prominent researcher, but conversational reply format limits reach.
items.summary_html: Andrew Wilson explains methodology for learning a shared human kernel from free-hand extrapolation data, treating each person's extrapolation as a sample from shared posterior
items.content_html: @roydanroy It's a good question. In this case, we treated each human free-hand extrapolation as a sample from a shared posterior. We were more interested in the similarities than differences between people, so this setup provided a special opportunity for pretty ambitious estimation of the human kernel, with a lot of statistical signal for learning a shared kernel from multiple draws. The objective for learning the human kernel looks like a product of posterior predictives for each person conditioned on a kernel. So this procedure would encompass people updating their kernel based on what they see. We looked at what happens to the learned human kernel as we increased the amount of available data, to also get a sense of prior biases.

items.title:
“Compute as a service.”

Wait I thought @SpaceX was a rocket company.

But last night I put up a vid...
items.content:
“Compute as a service.”

Wait I thought @SpaceX was a rocket company.

But last night I put up a video from @theresidency’s San Francisco startup demo day.

First company on the video is putting NVIDIA datacenters up in space.

“How you getting them up there?” I asked its founder.

“SpaceX.”

“How you connecting them to humans?”

“SpaceX.”

Wait a second. You know how Uber is a transportation as a service company but owns no vehicles?

SpaceX could rent out compute in space without owning any datacenters.

Highly profitable.

And, of course, Elon will have his own too.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer discusses NVIDIA datacenters in space, launched and connected via SpaceX. Compares to Uber model - SpaceX could rent compute in space without owning datacenters.
items.reasoning: Interesting futuristic concept about space-based compute infrastructure. From a startup demo day. Speculative but thought-provoking.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer discusses NVIDIA datacenters in space, launched and connected via SpaceX. Compares to Uber model - SpaceX could rent compute in space without owning datacenters.

items.title: @elizlaraki The head of Google R&D told me the reason they went with the small bubble car is because...

items.content:
Building an agent is hard:

• Implementation
• Infrastructure
• Integrations
• Security

Easy to build a demo, but hard to scale past that.

Base44 lets *anyone* build way better products than they could without AI, and one of the only ones that are constantly improving.

items.title:
𝙈𝙮 𝙧𝙚𝙫𝙞𝙚𝙬:

This is a very handy book with many specific concepts, examples, applications, and devel...
items.content:
𝙈𝙮 𝙧𝙚𝙫𝙞𝙚𝙬:

This is a very handy book with many specific concepts, examples, applications, and development ideas for developers interested in AI Coding (AKA Vibe Coding). I love the progression of numerous 5-minute and 10-minute drills, sprints, lessons, demonstrations, practical exercises, and hands-on practices presented throughout the book. This truly reinforces and materially supports the book's principal premise: "Everyone is a Programmer". Coding should be fun. This book definitely makes it fun.

Disclosure: the publisher provided me with a free review copy of the book.

items.title:
The future will be so fun.

We will walk around our photos and play with some objects we captured.

...
items.content:
The future will be so fun.

We will walk around our photos and play with some objects we captured.

Talked this morning with founder of @MergeVR and this future is what he is building.

A new way to learn, which he will present next month at an education conference.

Our kids will all have to face a choice: do they cheat or do they learn?

In 20 years what makes an interesting human? 

The ones who choose the learning path.

Just like today.

But we didn’t have powerful cheating tools to distract us.

items.title: I now use my new site https://t.co/4p4dzTDVN6 to find hotels when I travel and I quickly realized sm...
items.content:
I now use my new site https://t.co/4p4dzTDVN6 to find hotels when I travel and I quickly realized smaller privately owned boutique hotels are generally a way better experience than big hotel chains

Chains have to pay franchise fees and there's no responsibility when something goes wrong, the hotel owner doesn't really care because they just paid for the chain name/logo so it's their responsibility, while the actual chain also doesn't care because they have so many hotels, why would they?

Having an issue in a small boutique hotel is an actual issue for a hotel owner because they actually care about their product, it's their life's work usually

Also since they don't pay a franchise fee (which adds up to a lot), you get more value for your $$$

So I've added a Boutique hotel filter, so you can find those independently owned hotels and have better stays! And support local business too 😊

items.summary: Levelsio promotes his hotel search site with a new 'boutique hotel' filter, arguing independent hotels provide better value than chains due to no franchise fees and more owner accountability.

I now use my new site https://t.co/4p4dzTDVN6 to find hotels when I travel and I quickly realized smaller privately owned boutique hotels are generally a way better experience than big hotel chains

Chains have to pay franchise fees and there's no responsibility when something goes wrong, the hotel owner doesn't really care because they just paid for the chain name/logo so it's their responsibility, while the actual chain also doesn't care because they have so many hotels, why would they?

Having an issue in a small boutique hotel is an actual issue for a hotel owner because they actually care about their product, it's their life's work usually

Also since they don't pay a franchise fee (which adds up to a lot), you get more value for your $$$

So I've added a Boutique hotel filter, so you can find those independently owned hotels and have better stays! And support local business too 😊

items.title: I appreciate this sentiment, but I strongly disagree with it. What all research has shown is that th...
items.content: I appreciate this sentiment, but I strongly disagree with it. What all research has shown is that there is a *HUGE* psychological difference in people's minds between paying $0 and just a small nominal amount. When you don't pay anything for something, you end up not valuing it at all. As long as we collect revenue for the public services through individual taxation, paying even a small nominal amount by everyone gives everyone a sense of the stake in the system.
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Tunguz's highest-engagement post (688 likes, 64K views) arguing there's a huge psychological difference between paying $0 vs any nominal amount for public services, advocating small payments to give everyone a stake in the system.
items.reasoning: Extremely high engagement but not AI-related - discusses public service pricing psychology and taxation.
items.summary_html: Tunguz's highest-engagement post (688 likes, 64K views) arguing there's a huge psychological difference between paying $0 vs any nominal amount for public services, advocating small payments to give everyone a stake in the system.
items.content_html: I appreciate this sentiment, but I strongly disagree with it. What all research has shown is that there is a *HUGE* psychological difference in people's minds between paying $0 and just a small nominal amount. When you don't pay anything for something, you end up not valuing it at all. As long as we collect revenue for the public services through individual taxation, paying even a small nominal amount by everyone gives everyone a sense of the stake in the system.

items.content: Very well put. Agree with all of it wholeheartedly. And the last part of it really resonates with me. As someone who has walked away from playing status games, both involuntarily and voluntarily, in many different settings and in many different career arcs, I know full well how disorienting and depressing it can be for your sense of self worth.

items.summary_html: Tunguz reflecting on walking away from status games, discussing how disorienting it is for self-worth, in response to something he found well-articulated.
items.content_html: Very well put. Agree with all of it wholeheartedly. And the last part of it really resonates with me. As someone who has walked away from playing status games, both involuntarily and voluntarily, in many different settings and in many different career arcs, I know full well how disorienting and depressing it can be for your sense of self worth.

items.title:
‼️ Could large language models turn out to be the tech industry’s Vietnam? 

All In’s @jason argues ...
items.content:
‼️ Could large language models turn out to be the tech industry’s Vietnam? 

All In’s @jason argues below is that today’s students are speaking out against AI, just as students in the 60s and 70s spoke out against the Vietnam war.  

But I think teh analogy might run deeper. In that war, the US invested massively in a huge, disastrous campaign that drained resources and achieved nothing, fueled by arrogance; the war moved forward for years despite evidence that it wasn’t working as planned.

Could the multitrillion dollar investment in AI, burning money at unprecedented rates, and still struggling with hallucinations, unreliability and misalignment even after massive investments, turn out to be another epic arrogance-fueled mistake?

items.title:
The problem isn't productivity. It's context switching.
 
Every time you jump between apps, you lose...
items.content:
The problem isn't productivity. It's context switching.
 
Every time you jump between apps, you lose focus.
 
→ Draft an email 
→ Research a topic 
→ Summarize findings 
→ Rewrite for tone
 
That's 4 tools for one task. No wonder deep work feels impossible.

items.title: A German teenager called then-Chancellor Scholz a bastard in an obviously humorous tweet about the s...
items.content:
A German teenager called then-Chancellor Scholz a bastard in an obviously humorous tweet about the slow internet. The post got around ~500 views.

Scholz got offended, and the kid was formally accused of hate speech against a public official (there's a law like that in Germany, cool, huh?).

The kid agreed to remove the tweet and then posted a photo of the tweet and the accusation of a crime.

The new tweet has gotten millions of views only on the kid's official account and many more millions across the internet.

items.summary: Burkov shares a story about a German teenager accused of hate speech against Scholz, resulting in Streisand effect
items.reasoning: Off-topic - not related to AI/ML.

items.title:
It does yes

There is no top 10 hotels with more than 150 hotels in their chain

Kinda like Dunbar N...
items.content:
It does yes

There is no top 10 hotels with more than 150 hotels in their chain

Kinda like Dunbar Number

Over 150 and a hotel chain gets too big and once it hits 1000 hotels it becomes unmanageable and really goes to shit fast

Hotels can't scale, well good ones can't, by definition!

items.title: United States is probably the best example why every country should be trying to attract the best im...
items.content:
United States is probably the best example why every country should be trying to attract the best immigrants in the world

And at the same time be strict about keeping bad immigrants out or deporting them

What I hate most about European's fear to discriminate, is that we end up bringing in the wrong people, then really bad crime and ghettos happen, and now you force people to vote for right wing nationalists who actually are fully anti immigrant which isn't what we want at alll I think

The balanced view is in the middle

items.title:
The difference is the naive notion that all immigrants are equal in cost or benefit to society

My o...
items.content:
The difference is the naive notion that all immigrants are equal in cost or benefit to society

My opinion is that there's good and bad immigrants

I say that as a perpetual immigrant myself

In Europe we brought a lot of bad immigrants in and are now seeing the effects of that

Crime rates by country of origin that are now hundreds to thousands higher than natives, the data is very clear about this

Anyone outside Europe seems to agree with this basic idea, there's good and bad people, there's a difference, there's people that contribute and people make our countries worse with crime and welfare abuse 

As Europeans, we're raised with guilt about World War II in our education systems so our biggest fear is to discriminate and be labeled as racists

But we have to discriminate between good and bad people

The world has lots of bad people and for them there should be no place in Europe

@swombat The difference is you have the naive notion that all immigrants are equal in cost or benefit to society

My opinion is that there's good and bad immigrants

In Europe we brought a lot of bad immigrants in and are now seeing the effects of that

Crime rates by country of origin that are hundreds to thousands higher than natives

Anyone outside Europe seems to agree with this basic idea, except maybe half or more than half of Europeans

We're raised with guilt about World War II in our education systems so our biggest fear is to discriminate and be labeled as racists

But we have to discriminate between good and bad people

The world has lots of bad people and for them there should be no place in Europe and we should not allow them to stay in Europe!

@swombat The difference is you have the naive notion that all immigrants are equal in cost or benefit to society

My opinion is that there's good and bad immigrants

In Europe we brought a lot of bad immigrants in and are now seeing the effects of that

Crime rates by country of origin that are hundreds to thousands higher than natives

Anyone outside Europe seems to agree with this basic idea, except maybe half or more than half of Europeans

We're raised with guilt about World War II in our education systems so our biggest fear is to discriminate and be labeled as racists

But we have to discriminate between good and bad people

The world has lots of bad people and for them there should be no place in Europe and we should not allow them to stay in Europe!

It should be a choice we thought, real cutlery or takeaway style

Wild!
